stem: In a survey of [[n]] households, [[nb]] own a car. Among those car-owning households, [[nab]] also own a bicycle. A household is chosen at random from the car owners. What is the probability that it also owns a bicycle?
solution: Conditioning on car ownership restricts the sample space to the [[nb]] car owners, of whom [[nab]] also own a bicycle: \( P(\text{bike}\mid\text{car}) = \frac{[[nab]]}{[[nb]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by the full survey size instead of the [[nb]] car owners; that is \( P(\text{car and bike}) \), not the conditional probability.
This is \( P(\text{car}) \), the probability the conditioning event occurs, not the conditional probability.
This inverts the ratio, computing \( P(\text{car}\mid\text{bike}) \)-style backwards.
This is the probability a car owner does NOT own a bicycle, the complement.
This divides the both-owners by the car-only owners rather than by all car owners.
This divides by the non-car owners instead of the car owners.

stem: A box contains [[n]] raffle tickets, [[k]] of which are winning tickets. Two tickets are drawn at random, one after another, without replacement. Given that the first ticket drawn is a winning ticket, what is the probability that the second ticket drawn is also a winning ticket?
solution: Once a winning ticket is removed, [[k]] - 1 winning tickets remain among the [[n]] - 1 tickets left, so \( P(\text{2nd wins} \mid \text{1st wins}) = \dfrac{[[k]] - 1}{[[n]] - 1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores that the first winning ticket was removed and reuses the original probability.
This forgets to remove the drawn ticket from the winner count in the numerator.
This forgets to remove the drawn ticket from the total count in the denominator.
This is the probability the second ticket is NOT a winner, the complement of the requested event.
This adds one to the winner count instead of subtracting the ticket already drawn.
This divides by the remaining losing tickets instead of the remaining total tickets.

stem: Events \( A \) and \( B \) satisfy \( P(A) = \frac{[[a]]}{[[n]]} \), \( P(B) = \frac{[[c]]}{[[n]]} \), and \( P(A \cup B) = \frac{[[u]]}{[[n]]} \). What is \( P(A \mid B) \)?
solution: First recover the intersection with inclusion-exclusion: \( P(A \cap B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cup B) = \dfrac{[[a]] + [[c]] - [[u]]}{[[n]]} \). Then \( P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \dfrac{[[a]] + [[c]] - [[u]]}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( P(B) \) and reports \( P(A \cap B) \) itself instead of the conditional probability.
This skips inclusion-exclusion and just divides \( P(A) \) by \( P(B) \).
This computes the intersection count backwards, subtracting \( P(B) \)'s numerator from the union instead of using inclusion-exclusion correctly.
This divides the intersection count by [[a]] instead of [[c]], computing \( P(B \mid A) \) instead.
This is just \( P(A) \), ignoring the conditioning on \( B \) entirely.
This is just \( P(A \cup B) \), not a conditional probability at all.

stem: Events \( A \) and \( B \) are independent, with \( P(A) = \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) and \( P(B) = \frac{[[c]]}{[[d]]} \). What is \( P(A \mid B) \)?
solution: For independent events, conditioning on \( B \) does not change the probability of \( A \): \( P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \dfrac{P(A)P(B)}{P(B)} = P(A) = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( P(B) \) instead of \( P(A) \), mixing up which event's probability survives the conditioning.
This is \( P(A \cap B) \), the joint probability, not the conditional probability.
This divides \( P(A) \) by \( P(B) \) instead of recognizing the conditional simplifies to \( P(A) \) directly.
This adds the two probabilities instead of recognizing independence leaves \( P(A \mid B) = P(A) \).
This is the complement of \( P(A) \), not \( P(A) \) itself.
This subtracts \( P(B) \) from \( P(A) \), which is not a valid probability computation here.

stem: Suppose \( P(B) = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]} \) and \( P(A \cap B) = \dfrac{[[e]]}{[[g]]} \). What is \( P(A \mid B) \)?
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, computing \( P(B \mid A) \)-style backwards.
This multiplies the two probabilities instead of dividing.
This subtracts the probabilities instead of dividing them.
This reports \( P(A \cap B) \) itself, forgetting to divide by \( P(B) \).
This reports \( P(B) \) itself instead of the conditional probability.
This adds the two probabilities instead of dividing them.
